query: What should I cook tomorrow (Friday) and when?
response: For Friday, I recommend cooking 230 chickens total. Batch 1: Start at 09:30 (25 chickens, ready by 11:00 for lunch). Batch 2: Start at 11:00 (30 chickens, ready by 12:30). Batch 3: Start at 13:30 (15 chickens, ready by 15:00 for afternoon). Batch 4: Start at 15:30 (28 chickens, ready by 17:00 for dinner). Batch 5: Start at 17:00 (35 chickens, ready by 18:30 for dinner peak). Batch 6: Start at 18:30 (20 chickens, ready by 20:00 for late dinner). Based on historical data, Fridays average 220-228 chickens sold.
ground_truth: Friday is a peak day averaging 220-228 chickens sold. The plan should include 6-7 batches with the largest batches at 11:00-13:00 (lunch) and 17:00-19:00 (dinner). Total should be 225-240 chickens with buffer.

query: How many chickens should I cook on Monday?
response: Monday is our slowest day. Historical data shows Mondays average 150-162 chickens sold. I recommend cooking 165 total: Batch 1 at 09:30 (20 chickens), Batch 2 at 11:00 (22 chickens), Batch 3 at 15:30 (22 chickens), Batch 4 at 17:00 (25 chickens), with 10 held in reserve. Skip the late evening batch entirely.
ground_truth: Monday is the lowest volume day averaging 150-162 chickens. Plan should recommend 160-170 chickens with fewer batches than peak days. Late evening cooking should be minimized.

query: It's 2pm and we still have 15 chickens from the lunch batch. What should I do?
response: With 15 chickens at 2pm, here's what I recommend: 1) Reduce the next batch by 10 chickens since demand is soft. 2) If fewer than 5 sell by 3pm, offer a $2 discount to move inventory. 3) These chickens will stay acceptable until about 4pm — after that, consider pulling them. 4) Do NOT start the afternoon batch early; wait until you're down to 5 chickens.
ground_truth: Real-time adjustment should include: reduce next batch size, consider discounting if not selling, set a time limit for freshness (about 2 hours), and delay next batch until current stock is nearly sold. Recommendations should be actionable for store associates.

query: What was our waste percentage last week?
response: Based on the data from April 7-13, here's the weekly waste breakdown: Monday 10 wasted (6.1%), Tuesday 10 (5.7%), Wednesday 8 (4.4%), Thursday 8 (4.2%), Friday 7 (3.1%), Saturday 7 (2.9%), Sunday 7 (3.4%). Weekly average: 4.3%. The highest waste days were Monday and Tuesday — both rainy days with lower foot traffic.
ground_truth: The store averages approximately 4.7% waste rate. Weekly waste should be calculated from chickens_wasted / chickens_cooked. Monday-Tuesday typically have higher waste percentages. Weather correlation should be noted.

query: Saturday is expected to be rainy. Should I adjust my cooking plan?
response: Yes, adjust down. Normal Saturdays sell 225-238 chickens, but rainy Saturdays show roughly 10-15% lower traffic. I recommend: 1) Reduce total to ~205 chickens. 2) Delay the first big batch by 30 minutes. 3) Cut each batch by 3-4 chickens. 4) Keep a reserve of 15 raw chickens ready in case rain stops and traffic picks up. Historical data shows rainy days (April 1, 8, 15) had 158-170 sales vs normal 175-190.
ground_truth: Rainy days historically show lower sales. The plan should recommend reducing total quantities by 10-15%, referencing specific rainy day data points. Should also suggest keeping raw reserves for flexibility.

query: What are the peak selling hours?
response: Based on Zava's hourly sales data, the two peak windows are: Lunch peak: 11:00-13:00, with 12:00 being the single highest hour (25 chickens average). Dinner peak: 17:00-19:00, with 18:00 being the absolute peak (28 chickens average). The afternoon lull is 14:00-16:00 (6-10 chickens/hour). Early morning (06:00-09:00) and late evening (20:00-21:00) are the lowest periods.
ground_truth: Peak hours are 11:00-13:00 (lunch, peaking at 12:00) and 17:00-19:00 (dinner, peaking at 18:00). The afternoon lull is 14:00-16:00. Early morning and late evening are lowest volume.

query: How can we reduce our waste rate from 4.7% to below 3%?
response: Here's a 5-point waste reduction plan: 1) Smaller, more frequent batches — cook 15-20 per batch instead of 25-30. 2) Decision rules: if fewer than 8 sold by 2pm, skip the 3pm batch. 3) End-of-day strategy: last batch no later than 18:30, quantity based on 19:00+ demand only (~8-10). 4) Discount protocol: any chicken older than 90 minutes gets a $2 discount. 5) Track hourly sell-through and adjust next-day plan. This could save ~$280/month in wasted product.
ground_truth: Waste reduction should include: smaller batch sizes, decision rules for store associates, end-of-day cutoff timing, discount protocols for aging inventory, and tracking improvements. Financial impact should be quantified.

query: Compare Tuesday and Saturday sales patterns.
response: Tuesday averages 160-170 chickens vs Saturday averaging 225-238. Key differences: 1) Tuesday lunch peak is 21 chickens/hour vs Saturday's 33. 2) Tuesday dinner peak is 25/hour vs Saturday's 38. 3) Tuesday has almost no morning demand, Saturday starts selling by 7am. 4) Revenue: Tuesday ~$1,650 vs Saturday ~$2,350. 5) Waste rate: Tuesday 5.7% vs Saturday 2.9% — because Saturday demand is more predictable and stronger.
ground_truth: Tuesday is a low day (160-170 sold) and Saturday is peak (225-238 sold). Comparison should cover hourly patterns, revenue differences, and waste rate differences. Saturday has lower waste percentage despite higher volume.

query: We're running a promotion tomorrow — buy one get one half off. How should I adjust?
response: For a BOGO 50% off promotion, expect 20-30% higher demand. If tomorrow is a typical Wednesday (172-178 baseline), plan for 210-230 chickens. Adjustments: 1) Start first big batch 30 minutes earlier. 2) Increase each batch by 5-8 chickens. 3) Add an extra batch at 14:00 for afternoon promotion traffic. 4) Staff an extra person at the rotisserie counter. 5) Warning: promotion drives impulse buys at lunch — front-load 60% of volume before 2pm.
ground_truth: A BOGO promotion should increase expected demand by 20-30%. The plan should increase total quantity, adjust batch timing to be earlier, and note that promotions drive lunch impulse purchases more than dinner.

query: What's our cost per chicken and profit margin?
response: Based on the financial data: Raw chicken cost: ~$3.00/unit. Seasoning: ~$0.25/unit. Energy (cooking): ~$0.15/unit. Packaging: ~$0.20/unit. Total cost: ~$3.60/unit. Selling price: $10.00/unit. Gross margin: $6.40/unit (64%). After factoring waste (4.7%), effective margin drops to about $6.10/unit. Best margin day: Saturday ($1,499 net). Worst: Monday ($926 net).
ground_truth: Cost breakdown should include raw chicken (~$3.00), seasoning (~$0.25), energy (~$0.15), packaging (~$0.20). Total ~$3.60/unit. Selling price $10.00. Gross margin ~64%. Waste reduces effective margin. Best day is Saturday, worst is Monday.

query: Plan a Sunday cooking schedule.
response: Sunday averages 183-198 chickens sold. Here's the plan: Batch 1: 09:30 start, 20 chickens (ready 11:00). Batch 2: 10:45, 28 chickens (ready 12:15 — Sunday lunch is big). Batch 3: 12:15, 22 chickens (ready 13:45). Batch 4: 15:00, 14 chickens (ready 16:30 — afternoon family shoppers). Batch 5: 16:15, 24 chickens (ready 17:45). Batch 6: 17:30, 30 chickens (ready 19:00 — Sunday dinner peak). Batch 7: 19:00, 10 chickens (ready 20:30 — last batch, small). Total: 200 with 10% buffer.
ground_truth: Sunday averages 183-198 sold. Should plan 195-210 chickens. Sunday has a strong lunch peak and family dinner. Schedule should show 6-7 batches with specific times. The last batch should be small.

query: What happens if our rotisserie oven breaks down at noon?
response: Emergency protocol for noon oven failure: 1) You have roughly 20-30 cooked chickens from the morning batches. 2) These need to sell within 2 hours (by 2pm). 3) You'll miss ~65 chickens of afternoon/dinner demand (13:00-21:00). 4) Revenue impact: ~$650 lost. 5) Immediately: call maintenance, notify manager, put up 'limited supply' signage. 6) If backup oven available, fire it up — but capacity will be reduced. 7) Consider pre-carved chicken for sandwiches to extend inventory.
ground_truth: Equipment failure response should include: estimate of remaining cooked inventory and shelf life, projected lost sales from afternoon/dinner, revenue impact, and contingency actions like signage, maintenance call, and alternative product uses.

query: How does weather affect our daily chicken sales?
response: Weather has a clear impact on sales: Sunny/warm days (70°F+): sales increase 5-10% above baseline. Example: April 12 (Saturday, 78°F, sunny) sold 238. Rainy/cold days (<55°F): sales drop 10-15%. Example: April 1 (Tuesday, rainy 52°F) sold only 163. Moderate weather has minimal impact. The biggest factor is the combination of day-of-week AND weather — a rainy Saturday still outsells a sunny Monday. Recommendation: check weather forecast the night before and adjust batch sizes by ±15%.
ground_truth: Weather correlation should reference specific data points. Warm/sunny days increase sales 5-10%, rainy/cold days decrease 10-15%. Day of week is a stronger factor than weather. The plan should suggest adjusting batch sizes based on weather forecast.

query: Give me a waste report for the last 30 days.
response: 30-day waste report (March 18 - April 17): Total chickens cooked: 5,880. Total sold: 5,720. Total wasted: 280. Overall waste rate: 4.67%. Worst waste days: Tuesdays (avg 10.3 wasted, 5.9%), Mondays (avg 9.5 wasted, 5.8%). Best waste days: Saturdays (avg 8.5 wasted, 3.5%), Fridays (avg 8.5 wasted, 3.7%). Top waste causes: 1) End-of-day unsold (60% of waste), 2) Overcooked morning batches sitting too long (25%), 3) Overestimated slow-day demand (15%). Cost of waste: $280 × $3.60 = $1,008 in raw costs + lost revenue of $2,800.
ground_truth: 30-day waste report should show total numbers, waste percentage (~4.67%), breakdown by day of week, identification of worst/best days, root causes, and financial impact of waste.

query: Should we start cooking chickens at 6am when the store opens?
response: No. The data clearly shows 6am-8am demand is 0-3 chickens per hour. Starting cooking at 6am means the first batch is ready at 7:30am — and those chickens will sit unsold until the 10am-11am ramp. By then they're 2.5-3 hours old and approaching the waste threshold. Recommendation: First batch should go in at 09:00-09:30, ready by 10:30-11:00 just as demand picks up. This eliminates early morning waste entirely and saves 3-5 chickens per day (~$36-60/day in reduced waste).
ground_truth: Early morning (6-8am) has near-zero demand. First batch should start at 09:00-09:30, not at store opening. Early cooking leads to waste from chickens sitting too long. The answer should quantify savings from delayed start.

query: How many stockouts did we have yesterday and what was the impact?
response: Yesterday (Thursday April 17) had 2 stockout events: 1) 12:30pm — 15 minutes out, estimated 4 lost sales ($40 revenue). Cause: lunch batch was 5 chickens short of peak demand. 2) 18:15pm — 10 minutes out, estimated 3 lost sales ($30 revenue). Cause: dinner batch timing was slightly late. Total lost revenue: ~$70. Fix: increase lunch batch by 5 and start the dinner batch 15 minutes earlier.
ground_truth: Yesterday had 2 stockout events at 12:30 (15 min, 4 lost sales) and 18:15 (10 min, 3 lost sales). Total estimated lost revenue ~$70. Fixes should address batch sizing and timing.

query: What's the best day to run a chicken promotion?
response: Tuesday or Monday — here's why: 1) These are our lowest volume days (150-170 chickens). 2) A promotion could boost volume 20-30% without straining capacity. 3) The oven has spare capacity on these days. 4) Waste is highest on these days, so more demand means less waste. 5) Avoid promoting on Friday/Saturday — we're already at near-capacity and promotion would cause stockouts. Financial impact: A Tuesday BOGO could increase revenue by $400-500 while reducing waste from 10 to 4 chickens.
ground_truth: Best promotion days are Monday/Tuesday (lowest volume, highest waste, spare capacity). Promoting on Friday/Saturday risks stockouts. Promotions on slow days reduce waste and increase revenue.

query: Create a real-time decision card for store associates.
response:
ZAVA ROTISSERIE DECISION CARD:

⏰ BEFORE EACH BATCH:
- Count chickens in warmer. If >10, DELAY next batch 30 min.
- If <3 in warmer, START next batch immediately.

🍗 DURING LUNCH (11am-1pm):
- Target: never below 5 in warmer
- If selling >12/hour, add 5 extra to next batch
- If selling <8/hour, reduce next batch by 5

🌙 AFTER 7PM:
- If >8 chickens in warmer, do NOT start another batch
- If >5 at 8:30pm, mark down $2 each

📊 END OF DAY:
- Record total wasted for tomorrow's planning
- Note any stockout times
- Check tomorrow's weather forecast
ground_truth: Decision card should include simple if/then rules for store associates. Cover: batch timing decisions, lunch rush management, dinner rush management, end-of-day protocols, and recording keeping. Language should be simple and actionable.

query: How much money did we make last week?
response: Last week (April 7-13) financials: Monday: $1,550 revenue, $958 net margin. Tuesday: $1,650 revenue, $1,022 net. Wednesday: $1,750 revenue, $1,093 net. Thursday: $1,820 revenue, $1,138 net. Friday: $2,280 revenue, $1,435 net. Saturday: $2,380 revenue, $1,499 net. Sunday: $1,980 revenue, $1,243 net. WEEKLY TOTAL: Revenue $13,410, Net margin $8,388. Best day: Saturday. Cost of waste: $288 (~57 chickens × ~$5.05 effective cost).
ground_truth: Weekly revenue should total approximately $13,410 with net margin around $8,388. Should show daily breakdown. Saturday is the best day. Waste cost should be included.

query: We're expecting a local sports event on Wednesday. How should I prepare?
response: A local sports event on Wednesday will likely boost dinner traffic 25-40%. Normal Wednesday: 172-178 chickens. With event: plan for 215-240. Adjustments: 1) Increase dinner batches (5pm-7pm) by 40% — cook 35-40 per batch instead of 25. 2) Keep lunch batches normal — event impact is primarily post-3pm. 3) Add an extra batch at 16:00 for early event crowd. 4) Stock extra packaging and bags. 5) Consider having 20 raw chickens on standby for a 7pm emergency batch. 6) Plan for sales to drop sharply at event start time, then spike when it ends.
ground_truth: Sports events boost mainly dinner traffic by 25-40%. Should increase afternoon/dinner batches significantly while keeping lunch normal. Should suggest reserve inventory and note the pattern of sales dropping during the event then spiking after.
